titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary Davidson, 13 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara. James refers to the letters and telegrams about his minor injury; is not happy that Payne is sending out his riding breeches as he wanted to try them on when he got home. He asks that apples are not sent in food parcels 'as they don't arrive in very good condition'. He suggests instead tinned things like gelatin of chicken, brawn, sardines, kippered herring and sausage as they 'carry very well and make a welcome change from our rations which practically never vary'. He again criticises the reporting of his wound in the newspapers as it looks as if he orchestrated the publicity. He writes this is the first dry day which might help the trenches to dry out. There is a rumour that leave might be reinstated but he is not hopeful.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
13/3/16 My dearest Mother â Since
I wrote last I have received
several letters from you &
also one from May. I also
received the wire asking how
I was, but I knew you would
have my letter almost at once
telling you all about it &
in any case there is no use
wiring from here as it does
not go any quicker than a
letter. Would you please
thank May for her letter
as I cant possibly find time
for a separate reply. In your last letter you
say that Payne is sending out
my riding breeches,
but I never asked him
to do so. What I wanted
him to do was to put a
pair of riding breeches in
hand & have them ready
to try on when I do get
leave. There is no use
his sending them out without
trying on as they would be
sure to be wrong. I do
hope they have not already
been despatched. I think
Mother dear you might stop
sending out apples as they
dont arrive in very good
condition & instead send
me an occasional parcel
of tinned things like gelantine
of chicken, brown, sardines
etc. Kippered Herrings & sausages
(Palethorpes) also carry very well
& they make a welcome change
in our rations which practically
never vary. Would you please
tell Malcolmson when making
up their usual parcel to
include 1 tin of Coopers
marmelade instead of
sweets & to always send
a gingerbread cake instead
of varying the brand. I see that the home papers
made an awful fuss about
my wound & it sounds so
ridiculous to anyone who
knows what actually happened.
There are any number of
similar escapes every day
which are never spoken of
simply because they dont
necessitate attention from
a Dr. & when the papers
make so much out of
so little it looks to others
out here as if one had
written & made a great
story for self advertisement.
The cut is of course healed
up long ago & indeed I never
felt the least inconvenience
from it. This was the
first fine day we have had.
The sun was quite warm
& it made one feel quite
bright & cheery. If it only
continues for a little the
trenches will soon dry up
& that will be such a relief
for they are in a terrible state.
There is a rumour going round
that leave is to start again
soon but I am not counting
on it. Now I must close
up with fondest love to
all. Your loving son Jim
On Active Service
cd 19th March/16
Mrs. S.C. Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co Down Ireland
Js Davidson 13/3/16
